entries.notes: Selected the Study 1 ordinary first-order EFA for the derivation sample. Factor mapping follows printed columns: Factor 1=F1, Factor 2=F2, Factor 3=F3. Principal axis factoring with varimax rotation was reported; because varimax is orthogonal, latent factor correlations were set to 0. Reported unit-weighted subscale correlations were not extracted as latent factor correlations. Language was inferred as English because no translation was reported and the scale/paper were in English in a Canadian sample.

Selected the Study 2 ordinary first-order CFA for the university student sample. Factor mapping follows printed columns: F1, F2, F3. CFA correlations were taken from Table III above the diagonal as instructed by the table note. Estimation method was not explicitly reported beyond use of LISREL 7.16, so met is null. Country and language were inferred from the Canadian study context/authorship and absence of a reported translation.

Selected the Study 2 ordinary first-order CFA for the psychiatric out-patient sample. Factor mapping follows printed columns: F1, F2, F3. CFA correlations were taken from Table III below the diagonal as instructed by the table note. Estimation method was not explicitly reported beyond use of LISREL 7.16, so met is null. Country and language were inferred from the Canadian study context/authorship and absence of a reported translation.

Components 1, 2, and 3 were mapped to F1, F2, and F3 by printed column order. All printed cross-loadings, negative loadings, and non-bold loadings were extracted; boldface was treated only as salience. Varimax is orthogonal, so off-diagonal factor correlations were set to 0 under the orthogonal EFA/PCA override. Tables 3 and 4 report observed Pearson correlations among total TAS-20/subscale scores rather than latent correlations for the selected PCA solution and were not extracted as factor correlations. Country was inferred from recruitment at the College of William and Mary in Virginia; language was inferred as English because no translation was mentioned. Sex/female percentage was not reported; demographic details were reported only for 69 of 99 participants, and no female percentage was visible.

entries.sample_id: global sample (all five baseline samples combined)
entries.notes: Selected the explicitly reported global three-factor PCA solution for all five baseline samples combined because it is the only item-level TAS-20 loading table. The five individual samples have congruence statistics but no separate item-level loading matrices, so they were not extracted as separate samples. F1 maps to Difficulty Identifying Feelings, F2 to Difficulty Describing Feelings, and F3 to Externally-Oriented Thinking. The table has an item-number conflict: the row printed as '2. I have physical sensations...' was assigned to TAS-20 item 3 by wording, and the row '2. It is difficult for me...' was assigned to item 2. Orthogonal Procrustes rotation was used, so EFA/PCA factor correlations were set to 0. Female percentage and mean age were computed as weighted values from the five sample characteristics. Participant country was not explicitly reported for the combined sample; all subjects were described as Dutch-speaking and the administered version was Dutch.

entries.notes: Selected the ordinary exploratory principal components solution for the whole sample, reported as a two-factor varimax-rotated solution in Table 2. Factor 1 maps to F1 and factor 2 maps to F2 by printed column order. The paper also reports split-half rotations of 2-, 3-, and 4-factor solutions used to decide the number of factors, but item-level loadings are presented for the whole-sample two-factor solution. Because the selected EFA/PCA solution used varimax rotation, the factor correlation R1.2 was set to 0 under the orthogonal EFA override. Female percentage was taken from the table's reported percentage among participants with sex indicated; 17 participants did not indicate sex.

Item 10 loading 2.08 and item 15 loading -0.029 were recorded exactly as printed. Age was reported for the initially recruited 390 students, while final analytic n was 383 after excluding seven cases with missing data.

Estimation method was not explicitly reported beyond CFA conducted in SPSS Amos, so met is null. F1 maps to Difficulty identifying feelings; F2 maps to Difficulty describing feelings; F3 maps to Externally oriented thinking. Table 3 reports only item parameters on their specified factors; nonreported cross-loadings are coded null. Estimation method was not explicitly reported, so met is null. Alternative CFA models removing Factor 3 or negatively keyed items were ignored because the full three-factor all-item CFA is the highest-dimensional complete CFA scale model reported with item-level parameters.

Selected the ordinary first-order CFA three-factor TAS-20-S model. F1 maps to Difficulty identifying feelings; F2 maps to Difficulty describing feelings; F3 maps to Externally oriented thinking. Table 3 reports only item parameters on their specified factors; nonreported cross-loadings are coded null. The negative loading for item 18 on F3 was extracted as printed. Estimation method was not explicitly reported, so met is null. Alternative CFA models removing Factor 3 or negatively keyed items were ignored because the full three-factor all-item CFA is the highest-dimensional complete CFA scale model reported with item-level parameters; the authors caution that F3 coefficients in the Peruvian sample may reflect suppressor/spurious effects.

entries.notes: Selected the 5-factor exploratory PCA/ACP solution with varimax rotation from Table I. A 6-factor PCA solution and CFA models are described, but the 5-factor loading matrix is the reported item-level table and is described as retained/better; CFA alternatives did not provide item-level loading matrices. Because the selected EFA uses varimax rotation, factor correlations were set to 0 under the orthogonal EFA rule. A second exploratory analysis on 143 subjects excluding depressed participants is described as comparable, but no item-level loading table is reported, so it was not extracted. Age mean was not reported; only an 18-60 year age range was visible. Country was inferred from recruitment at the CHU de Toulouse.

entries.notes: Selected the ordinary first-order CFA three-factor TAS-20 model because the paper tested one-, two-, and three-factor CFA models for the same sample and the three-factor model was the highest-dimensional eligible CFA model. Factor mapping follows the table columns: F1 = DIF, F2 = DDF, F3 = EOT. Table 1 reports only one loading per item under its assigned factor; all nonprinted cross-loadings were coded null. Latent factor correlations for the selected CFA model were not reported; Pearson correlations among TAS-20 subscale scores in the text were not extracted as latent factor correlations. Estimation method for CFA was not reported. Language was inferred as English because participants were recruited in Australia and no translation/version was mentioned.
